mission.title: Install and Configure Trivy Operator on Kubernetes
mission.description: Trivy Operator is the Kubernetes-native deployment of the Trivy vulnerability scanner from Aqua Security. It runs continuously inside the cluster, scans every Pod's container images, configurations, and exposed secrets, and writes the findings to Custom Resources such as VulnerabilityReport, ConfigAuditReport, ExposedSecretReport, and SbomReport. Platform teams use the Operator to surface CVEs, hardening misconfigurations, and exposed secrets without standing up an external scanning pipeline. This mission installs the Operator, demonstrates a vulnerability scan against a deliberately outdated workload, and covers the production hardening (resource limits, scan scope, integrations with Prometheus and external SIEMs) that real workloads require.

steps.title: Add the Aqua Security Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Aqua Security Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add aqua https://aquasecurity.github.io/helm-charts/
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the Trivy Operator
steps.description:
Install the Trivy Operator into a dedicated 'trivy-system' namespace. The chart installs the Operator Deployment plus the CRDs for VulnerabilityReport, ConfigAuditReport, ExposedSecretReport, RbacAssessmentReport, ClusterRbacAssessmentReport, ClusterComplianceReport, ClusterInfraAssessmentReport, ClusterSbomReport, and SbomReport.
```bash
helm install trivy-operator aqua/trivy-operator --version 0.32.1 \
  --namespace trivy-system --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Operator pod is running and the CRDs are registered.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace trivy-system
kubectl get crd | grep aquasecurity.github.io
```

steps.title: Deploy a sample workload to scan
steps.description:
Create a dedicated 'trivy-demo' namespace and deploy a deliberately outdated nginx Deployment so the Operator has something to scan. The Operator watches Pods cluster-wide by default and produces a VulnerabilityReport per workload-controller within ~30-60 seconds. Using a dedicated namespace keeps the demo workload isolated from real workloads and gives a single-command teardown.
```bash
kubectl create namespace trivy-demo
kubectl create deployment vulnerable-nginx --image=nginx:1.18 --namespace trivy-demo
kubectl rollout status deployment/vulnerable-nginx --namespace trivy-demo --timeout=60s
```

steps.title: View the scan results
steps.description:
Once the scan job completes, the Operator writes a VulnerabilityReport into the same namespace as the scanned workload. List the reports and look at the summary on one of them.
```bash
kubectl get vulnerabilityreports --all-namespaces
kubectl get vulnerabilityreport --namespace trivy-demo -o jsonpath='{.items[0].report.summary}'
```
For a full list of CVEs, query the report directly:
```bash
kubectl get vulnerabilityreport --namespace trivy-demo -o yaml
```

steps.title: Restrict scan scope (recommended for production)
steps.description:
By default the Operator scans every namespace. On large clusters this can be expensive. Set 'targetNamespaces' to a comma-separated list to scope scanning to specific namespaces, or 'excludeNamespaces' to skip noisy ones (such as kube-system).
```bash
helm upgrade trivy-operator aqua/trivy-operator --version 0.32.1 \
  --namespace trivy-system \
  --reuse-values \
  --set 'trivy.targetNamespaces=default\,production\,staging' \
  --set 'trivyOperator.excludeNamespaces=kube-system\,trivy-system'
```

steps.title: Enable the Prometheus metrics endpoint
steps.description:
The Operator exposes Prometheus metrics on port 8080 of the Operator pod. Enable a ServiceMonitor (Prometheus Operator) or scrape the endpoint directly to track findings count, scan duration, and report freshness.
```bash
helm upgrade trivy-operator aqua/trivy-operator --version 0.32.1 \
  --namespace trivy-system \
  --reuse-values \
  --set 'serviceMonitor.enabled=true'
```
For a quick check via port-forward:
```bash
kubectl port-forward -n trivy-system deploy/trivy-operator 8080:8080
curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:8080/metrics | grep trivy_image_vulnerabilities | head -5
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Set resource limits on the Operator pod and on the scanner job pods that the Operator launches. Scan jobs run downloads-and-runs, so they need ephemeral storage and CPU bursts.
```bash
helm upgrade trivy-operator aqua/trivy-operator --version 0.32.1 \
  --namespace trivy-system \
  --reuse-values \
  --set 'operator.replicas=1' \
  --set 'operator.resources.requests.cpu=100m' \
  --set 'operator.resources.requests.memory=128Mi' \
  --set 'operator.resources.limits.cpu=500m' \
  --set 'operator.resources.limits.memory=512Mi' \
  --set 'trivy.resources.requests.cpu=100m' \
  --set 'trivy.resources.requests.memory=256Mi' \
  --set 'trivy.resources.limits.cpu=1' \
  --set 'trivy.resources.limits.memory=1Gi'
```

uninstall.title: Remove the sample workload
uninstall.description:
Remove the demo namespace so the Operator can drain its scan queue cleanly. Deleting the namespace removes the demo Deployment and its associated VulnerabilityReports in one step.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace trivy-demo
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Trivy Operator release.
```bash
helm uninstall trivy-operator --namespace trivy-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and namespace
uninstall.description:
Removing the CRDs deletes any remaining VulnerabilityReport, ConfigAuditReport, and other report resources cluster-wide. Some chart versions auto-clean their CRDs on uninstall, in which case the delete prints NotFound and that is safe to ignore.
```bash
kubectl delete crd vulnerabilityreports.aquasecurity.github.io
kubectl delete crd clustervulnerabilityreports.aquasecurity.github.io
kubectl delete crd configauditreports.aquasecurity.github.io
kubectl delete crd clusterconfigauditreports.aquasecurity.github.io
kubectl delete crd exposedsecretreports.aquasecurity.github.io
kubectl delete crd rbacassessmentreports.aquasecurity.github.io
kubectl delete crd clusterrbacassessmentreports.aquasecurity.github.io
kubectl delete crd infraassessmentreports.aquasecurity.github.io
kubectl delete crd clusterinfraassessmentreports.aquasecurity.github.io
kubectl delete crd clustercompliancereports.aquasecurity.github.io
kubectl delete crd sbomreports.aquasecurity.github.io
kubectl delete crd clustersbomreports.aquasecurity.github.io
kubectl delete namespace trivy-system
```

upgrade.title: Backup current values
upgrade.description:
Export the current Helm values before upgrading.
```bash
helm get values trivy-operator --namespace trivy-system > trivy-operator-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Refresh the Aqua Security Helm repository to pick up the latest chart.
```bash
helm repo update
helm search repo aqua/trivy-operator --versions | head -5
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Trivy Operator
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the release. Always read the chart CHANGELOG for breaking changes between versions, especially around CRD schema changes that may require a re-scan to populate.
```bash
helm upgrade trivy-operator aqua/trivy-operator --namespace trivy-system --version 0.32.1 --reuse-values
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Operator deployment rolled out and is running the new image, and that existing reports remain queryable.
```bash
kubectl rollout status deploy/trivy-operator -n trivy-system
kubectl get vulnerabilityreports --all-namespaces
```

troubleshooting.title: Operator pod in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
The most common cause is missing RBAC for the watched namespaces or a misconfigured value pointing at a non-existent ConfigMap. Check the Operator logs.
```bash
kubectl logs -n trivy-system deploy/trivy-operator --tail=200
```

troubleshooting.title: No VulnerabilityReport produced after deploying a workload
troubleshooting.description:
If a workload exists but no report appears within a couple of minutes, the scan job itself may be stuck. Look at the scan jobs in the workload's namespace and the Operator's reconciler logs.
```bash
kubectl get jobs --namespace trivy-demo
kubectl describe job --namespace trivy-demo $(kubectl get jobs --namespace trivy-demo -o name | head -1)
kubectl logs -n trivy-system deploy/trivy-operator --tail=200 | grep -i scan
```

troubleshooting.title: Scan jobs fail with image-pull or DB-download errors
troubleshooting.description:
Scan jobs and the Operator's policy loader need cluster egress to a container registry that mirrors the Trivy DB and policy bundles (the chart default is 'mirror.gcr.io', with 'ghcr.io/aquasecurity/trivy-db' as the upstream alternative). On kind clusters or restricted-egress networks where one of these is unreachable, scan jobs CrashLoopBackOff with 'failed to download vulnerability DB' and the Operator log shows 'connection refused' against the configured mirror. Pre-mirror the database to a registry your cluster can reach and override 'trivy.dbRepository'.
```bash
kubectl describe job --namespace trivy-demo $(kubectl get jobs --namespace trivy-demo -o name | head -1) | tail -20
helm get values trivy-operator --namespace trivy-system | grep -A2 dbRepository
```
For air-gapped clusters, mirror 'ghcr.io/aquasecurity/trivy-db' and 'ghcr.io/aquasecurity/trivy-java-db' to your registry and override both 'trivy.dbRepository' and 'trivy.javaDbRepository' on install.

troubleshooting.title: VulnerabilityReports are empty (no CVEs reported)
troubleshooting.description:
If reports are produced but contain no findings, the scan completed but found nothing relevant for the configured severity threshold. Check the Operator config for severity filters.
```bash
helm get values trivy-operator --namespace trivy-system | grep -i severity
kubectl get vulnerabilityreport --namespace trivy-demo -o jsonpath='{.items[0].report}' | head -100
```

troubleshooting.title: High CPU usage in trivy-system namespace
troubleshooting.description:
The Operator schedules one scan job per workload-controller. On large clusters the parallelism can saturate node CPU. Tune 'trivyOperator.concurrentScanJobsLimit' to throttle concurrent scans.
```bash
kubectl top pods -n trivy-system --all-namespaces | head -20
helm get values trivy-operator --namespace trivy-system | grep -A2 concurrent
```

resolution.summary: A successful production install of Trivy Operator has the Operator pod Running in 'trivy-system', VulnerabilityReports being produced for every Pod-spec in the configured target namespaces within a minute of workload creation, the Prometheus metrics endpoint scraped (or a ServiceMonitor enabled if Prometheus Operator is in use), scan scope tuned to exclude noisy namespaces, and resource limits set on both the Operator and the scanner job pods. From there reports can be queried via kubectl, exported to a SIEM, or wired into a Grafana dashboard via the Prometheus metrics.

prerequisites.description: Helm and kubectl must be installed locally. The Operator's scan jobs need cluster egress to the Trivy database registry (ghcr.io/aquasecurity/trivy-db) and to the container registries hosting your workload images. For air-gapped clusters, mirror both into a registry you control and override 'trivy.dbRepository' on install.
